mainEntity.name: How much does a Sprinter engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A Sprinter engine replacement costs £1,500–£7,000 supply-only depending on the generation and engine — an OM651 four-cylinder diesel for the NCV3/VS30 is £2,800–£4,800 [MB-QUOTE], an OM642 V6 diesel is £3,200–£5,500 [MB-QUOTE], and an OM602 for the T1N is £1,500–£2,800 [MB-QUOTE]. Labour adds 8–12 hours for four-cylinder units, 10–14 hours for V6 units, at £65–£95/hr (independent commercial van specialist) or £110–£160/hr (Mercedes-Benz dealer). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised estimate.

mainEntity.name: Which Sprinter engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM651 2.1-litre four-cylinder diesel — fitted to the NCV3 (2009–2018) and early VS30 Sprinters — is the most reliable Sprinter engine. It generated 1,250 UK enquiries in 2025, most of which relate to EGR valve failures, fuel filter housing coolant leaks and glow plug relay issues — all well-understood, manageable repairs [MB-VERIFIED]. The OM651 is the engine that made the Sprinter the UK's favourite van — its weaknesses are known, predictable and cheaply fixed.

mainEntity.name: Which Sprinter engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: Two caveats: Early OM611/OM612 CDI engines in the T1N — the injectors are prone to failure, and budget £300–£800 per injector to replace them. OM642 V6 diesel — it's not unreliable, but it's more expensive to maintain and repair than the OM651. The oil cooler seal leak is a labour-intensive repair (£800–£1,500) and the V6 consumes more fuel. Only choose it if you genuinely need the extra torque for heavy towing or payload.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my Sprinter engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Concrete symptoms by engine: OM651 diesel (NCV3/VS30) — EGR valve failure (limp mode, loss of power), fuel filter housing coolant leak (coolant loss with no visible leak), glow plug relay failure (starting issues). OM642 V6 diesel — oil leak from the 'V' of the engine (oil cooler seals), coolant loss with no visible leak, or oil in the coolant. OM611/OM612 CDI (T1N) — rough running, smoke, loss of power, injector failure. OM602 (T1N) — virtually indestructible; if rough, check injection pump and fuel system first.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Sprinter engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 70% rule: if a reconditioned engine costs 70% or more of your van's current market value, get a scrap valuation before committing. But for commercial vehicles, also factor in down-time costs and lost revenue. T1N (OM602) — 30–70% — borderline to viable. T1N (OM611/OM612) — 50–80% — often not worth it. NCV3 (OM651) — 25–70% — usually worth it for well-maintained examples. NCV3 (OM642) — 25–80% — watch the numbers, but often viable for fleet operators. VS30 (OM654) — 8–30% — worth replacing.

mainEntity.name: Which Sprinter generation should I buy?

acceptedAnswer.text: Buy a 2013–2018 NCV3 with the OM651 2.1 CDI — it's the most reliable, best-supported Sprinter you can own. The 2013 facelift brought improved electronics and the OM651 in its most refined form. If you need a V6, choose the OM642 but budget for an oil cooler seal inspection (£800–£1,500) before purchase. For a VS30, choose the OM654 (2021+) and insist on full service history — the early OM651s in the VS30 are the same proven engine but without the latest tech.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Sprinter engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Check the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen or on the driver's door pillar) — the engine code is encoded in positions 4–8 of the VIN. The vehicle registration document (V5C) lists the engine number in Section 4. Under-bonnet sticker on the slam panel or timing cover often shows the engine code. If unsure, use our diagnostic calculator to help identify it.

mainEntity.name: Sprinter vs Vito — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: They're different classes — the Sprinter is a large van (3.5–5.5 tonnes), the Vito is a medium van (2.8–3.5 tonnes). They share some engines — the OM651 is used in both — but the Sprinter is generally heavier-duty and more durable. The Vito's OM651 is the same engine but in a lighter chassis with less towing capacity. For reliability, choose the Sprinter if you need the payload and durability; choose the Vito if you need a smaller van for city work.

hero.h1: Sprinter Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every Sprinter engine — from the T1N's OM602 diesel to the VS30's OM654 — across five generations of Mercedes‑Benz's iconic commercial van. Real UK costs, reliability data and ownership verdicts for the T1N (1995–2006), NCV3 (2006–2018) and VS30 (2018–present). Backed by real UK enquiry data from Mercedes‑Benz owners in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ✅ The Sprinter rule: commercial value changes everything — a £8,000–£12,000 OM642 recon against a £5,000–£10,000 van is borderline; against a £30,000+ fleet van it's a no‑brainer. Factor down‑time and MOT viability into every decision
OM651 — the 2.1‑litre four‑cylinder diesel; 1,250 UK enquiries in 2025, well‑understood weaknesses and proven in high‑mileage fleet use [MB-VERIFIED]
OM642 — the 3.0‑litre V6 diesel; oil cooler seal leaks are the primary weakness, labour‑intensive but rarely catastrophic; 880 UK enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED]

NCV3 (2006–2018) with OM651 2.1 CDI — the most common, best‑supported Sprinter engine; parts are cheap, specialists are everywhere, and the engines regularly exceed 300,000 miles with proper maintenance
OM651 EGR valve and injector issues — 1,250 UK enquiries in 2025 across Sprinter and passenger‑car models [MB-VERIFIED]
🏆 The UK's favourite workhorse — the Sprinter is the definitive Mercedes‑Benz commercial van, with a reputation for longevity that's unmatched in the class

OM642 — 3.0‑litre V6 diesel; oil cooler seal leaks are the primary concern
H2: Sprinter Generations — Find Your Vehicle

rows.metric: Sub-headline: Five generations, three distinct chassis codes — from the original T1N that defined the modern van to the tech‑loaded VS30.

Our Verdict: The original Sprinter — the van that defined the modern large‑van segment. The OM602 2.9‑litre five‑cylinder is virtually indestructible; the OM611/OM612 CDI units are more refined but have injector and fuel‑system weaknesses. Values are low — a £2,000–£4,000 engine replacement can easily exceed the van's value. Buy only as a keeper or project, or if you need a cheap commercial workhorse and are prepared to maintain it yourself.
Second Generation — NCV3 (2006–2018) 🔥 Most Enquired — data aggregated from shared engines [MB-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The NCV3 is the Sprinter sweet spot — the OM651 2.1 CDI is the engine to seek: proven, parts‑rich, and capable of 300,000+ miles with proper maintenance. The OM642 V6 offers more power but higher running costs and the oil‑cooler‑seal caveat. The 2013 facelift brought improved electronics and the OM651 in its most refined form. This is the best used Sprinter you can buy.

rows.metric: [2018–present] • OM651 2.1 CDI (early) • OM654 2.0 CDI (2021+) • OM642 3.0 V6 (early, phased out)

Our Verdict: The modern VS30 is larger, more refined and packed with technology — but the complexity is real. Early VS30s (2018–2021) still use the proven OM651 and OM642 engines. The 2021 update replaced both with the single OM654 four‑cylinder diesel — more efficient and cleaner, but untested in high‑mileage fleet use. Buy new or nearly‑new with warranty; used examples need a thorough specialist inspection.

engineDatabase.h2: Sprinter Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every verified Sprinter engine code — generation, fuel, power, reliability rating and real UK enquiry data.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM651, OM642 and OM654 ratings: 95% confidence — based on 1,250, 880 and 520 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries respectively, cross‑referenced with Mercedes TSBs and specialist interviews. OM602 rating: Expert‑knowledge tier — legendary durability, documented across thousands of fleet vehicles [PATTERN DATA]. OM611, OM612, OM646 and M111 ratings: Expert‑knowledge tier — documented failure patterns and specialist interviews, not proprietary volume data [PATTERN DATA]. Data note (internal): Correction applied: OM651 was incorrectly listed in some sources as a 2.2‑litre unit — verified as 2,143cc (2.1‑litre) for the Sprinter, with the OM646 as the earlier 2.1‑litre CDI. [Fix at source: update engine displacement fields.] Correction applied: OM654 in the Sprinter is the 1,950cc variant (not the 1,993cc passenger‑car version) — introduced in 2021 to replace both OM651 and OM642. [Fix at source: update engine displacement and introduction year.] Correction applied: OM642 and OM651 were both available in the VS30 until 2021, when the OM654 replaced them. The OM642 V6 was discontinued in the Sprinter with the 2021 update. [Fix at source: update generation coverage.] No further corrections needed — source data matched live verification for all other engine codes.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six most common Sprinter failure patterns — from OM651 EGR issues to OM642 oil cooler seals — ranked by severity.

problems.description: The EGR valve on the 2.1‑litre diesel is prone to clogging and failure, causing limp mode and loss of power. NCV3 and early VS30 Sprinters affected. Common fault code P040000. £400–£1,200 repair [MB-QUOTE].

problems.issue: OM651 fuel filter housing coolant leak
problems.description: The fuel filter housing has a coolant pipe through it that leaks at the joint — a very common OM651 weakness. NCV3 Sprinters affected. £300–£800 repair [MB-QUOTE].

The V6 diesel's primary weak point — an O‑ring fails in the "V" of the engine, requiring significant disassembly to access. NCV3 and early VS30 Sprinters with OM642 affected. £800–£1,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].

A failure of the glow plug relay can cause an electrical short circuit and overheating of the component. NCV3 Sprinters (2013–2018 builds) affected — 114,055 vehicles recalled. £200–£500 repair [MB-QUOTE].

AdBlue system issues including tank level/temperature sensor failure, crystallised dosing injectors, and NOx sensor faults. VS30 Sprinters with OM651 and OM654 affected. £400–£1,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].
Early CDI injectors are prone to failure, causing rough running, smoke and loss of power. T1N Sprinters affected. £300–£800 per injector [MB-QUOTE].

marketIntelligence.h2: Sprinter Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025
insights: "Interesting insight:" The OM651 2.1‑litre four‑cylinder diesel — fitted to the NCV3 and early VS30 Sprinters — generated 1,250 UK enquiries in 2025, making it the most‑enquired Mercedes diesel of the year [MB-VERIFIED]. The vast majority relate to EGR valve failures, fuel filter housing coolant leaks and glow plug relay issues — all well‑understood, manageable repairs. The OM651 is the engine that made the Sprinter the UK's favourite van; its weaknesses are known, predictable and cheaply fixed.

The OM642 V6 diesel — fitted to the NCV3 and early VS30 Sprinters — generated 880 UK enquiries in 2025, with the oil cooler seal leak accounting for a significant proportion [MB-VERIFIED]. The V6 offers more power but higher running costs — and the oil cooler seal repair is labour‑intensive (£800–£1,500). For most fleet operators, the OM651 is the smarter choice; the OM642 is for those who genuinely need the extra torque for heavy towing or payload.

The OM654 — introduced in the 2021 VS30 update — generated 520 UK enquiries in 2025, reflecting its relatively recent introduction. Early data suggests it's reliable, but it's too early for high‑mileage fleet data. The OM654 replaces both the OM651 and OM642 in the Sprinter range — a bold move that reduces complexity but puts all Sprinter eggs in one engine basket.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every Sprinter Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: Buy an NCV3 (2009–2018) with the OM651 2.1 CDI — it's the most reliable, best‑supported Sprinter you can own. The EGR valve and fuel filter housing are known, cheaply‑fixed weaknesses, not reasons to avoid the van. If you need a V6, budget for an oil cooler seal inspection (£800–£1,500) and factor the higher running costs into your commercial calculation. For a VS30, choose the OM654 (2021+) and insist on full service history — the early OM651s in the VS30 are the same proven engine but without the latest tech.

replacementCosts.h2: Sprinter Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for every Sprinter engine — used, reconditioned and rebuilt supply-only costs, with independent labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a Sprinter engine typically takes 8–12 hours for four‑cylinder units, 10–14 hours for V6 units, at £65–£95/hr (independent commercial van specialist) or £110–£160/hr (Mercedes‑Benz dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. The Sprinter rule — commercial value changes everything: A £2,800–£4,800 OM651 recon against an £4,000–£12,000 NCV3 sits at 40–70% of the van's value — borderline on lower-value examples, safe on better vans. But the commercial calculation is different: down-time, lost revenue, and the cost of replacing the van all factor in. A £3,200–£5,500 OM642 recon against an £18,000–£40,000 VS30 sits at 10–25% — well within the 70% threshold. For T1N vans, a £1,500–£3,500 recon against a £1,500–£5,000 van is often terminal — unless the van has sentimental or low‑mileage collector value. In short: fix an NCV3 with a known engine, budget carefully for a T1N, and treat a VS30 as a modern asset worth repairing.

The Sprinter rule — commercial value changes everything: A £2,800–£4,800 OM651 recon against an £4,000–£12,000 NCV3 sits at 40–70% of the van's value — borderline on lower-value examples, safe on better vans. But the commercial calculation is different: down-time, lost revenue, and the cost of replacing the van all factor in. A £3,200–£5,500 OM642 recon against an £18,000–£40,000 VS30 sits at 10–25% — well within the 70% threshold. For T1N vans, a £1,500–£3,500 recon against a £1,500–£5,000 van is often terminal — unless the van has sentimental or low‑mileage collector value. In short: fix an NCV3 with a known engine, budget carefully for a T1N, and treat a VS30 as a modern asset worth repairing.

engineEvolution.h2: How the Sprinter Engine Range Evolved — 1995 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes launched the Sprinter as the successor to the T1/TN van — a clean‑sheet design that would define the modern large‑van segment. The OM602 2.9‑litre five‑cylinder diesel was the standout engine — virtually indestructible and capable of 500,000+ miles with basic maintenance
eras.worthKnowing: The OM602 is legendary — it's the engine that built the Sprinter's reputation for longevity. Many are still on the road today with original engines. Parts are cheap and widely available

eras.keyEngines: OM611 2.1 CDI, OM612 2.7 CDI, M111 2.3 petrol
eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes introduced common‑rail diesel (CDI) to the Sprinter — the OM611 and OM612 replaced the older indirect‑injection engines. More refined, more powerful, but with more complex fuel systems and injector weaknesses. The M111 petrol was a niche option for markets where diesel wasn't favoured
eras.worthKnowing: The OM611/OM612 injectors are the primary weakness — they fail and can be expensive to replace. Budget £300–£800 per injector if buying a T1N CDI

eras.keyEngines: OM646 2.1 CDI, OM651 2.1 CDI, OM642 3.0 V6 CDI
eras.whyBmwChanged: The NCV3 brought a larger body, improved safety, and a range of new engines. The OM646 was a development of the OM611; the OM651 (introduced in 2009) was the game‑changer — more reliable, more efficient, and capable of 300,000+ miles. The OM642 V6 offered more power for heavy‑duty applications
eras.worthKnowing: The OM651 is the Sprinter engine to buy — it's the most common, best‑supported, and proven in fleet use. The 2013 facelift brought improved electronics and the OM651 in its most refined form

eras.keyEngines: OM651 2.1 CDI (early), OM642 3.0 V6 (early), OM654 2.0 CDI (2021+)
eras.whyBmwChanged: The VS30 is larger, more luxurious and packed with technology. Early VS30s kept the OM651 and OM642 — proven engines in a new body. In 2021, Mercedes replaced both with a single OM654 four‑cylinder diesel — more efficient, cleaner, but untested in high‑mileage fleet use
eras.worthKnowing: The 2021 update was a major change — the OM651 and OM642 are now discontinued in the Sprinter. The OM654 is a good engine but it's too early for long‑term data. For fleet operators, the OM654 may prove to be a worthy successor; for private buyers, the OM651 remains the safer bet

eras.era: SECTION 10 — WHO SHOULD BUY A SPRINTER? — <WhoShouldBuy>
eras.years: H2: Who Should Buy a Sprinter?

eras.keyEngines: The Sprinter is the UK's favourite large van for a reason — the OM651 NCV3 is the fleet champion. Buy 2013–2018 NCV3s with OM651 engines, maintain them strictly, and they'll deliver 300,000+ miles with predictable, manageable repairs. Avoid the OM642 V6 unless you genuinely need the extra torque for heavy towing or payload.

eras.era: An NCV3 Sprinter with the OM651 2.1 CDI is a solid investment — it's reliable, parts are cheap, and every specialist in the country knows how to fix it. Budget for an EGR valve and fuel filter housing inspection before purchase; factor £400–£1,200 for preventative replacements. A VS30 is more expensive to buy and maintain — only upgrade if your business can justify the cost.
eras.whyBmwChanged: The Sprinter is a campervan conversion favourite — the NCV3 is the sweet spot: robust, spacious, and the OM651 engine is proven. Pre‑2013 NCV3s are cheaper but have the OM646 engine (more injector issues); 2013–2018 is the conversion sweet spot. Budget for an EGR and injector check before the conversion starts.

eras.years: A T1N Sprinter is the cheapest way into a large van — but a £1,500–£4,000 T1N with an OM602 engine is a project, not a daily driver. Buy it if you can work on it yourself or if you need a cheap occasional workhorse. For reliable daily use, an NCV3 with the OM651 is worth the extra £2,000–£4,000 purchase price.
eras.worthKnowing: A 2013–2018 NCV3 Sprinter with the OM651 2.1 CDI is an excellent first van — it's reliable, easy to drive, and you'll find specialists everywhere. Buy on condition and service history, not mileage. Avoid the OM642 V6 (higher fuel and repair costs) and avoid T1N vans unless you're a confident diy‑mechanic.

eras.years: H2: Can I Repair My Sprinter Engine — or Should I Replace It?
eras.keyEngines: If you've got a T1N Sprinter with an OM611/OM612 CDI engine, the numbers are brutal — a £1,800–£3,500 recon against a £1,500–£5,000 van means replacement cost can exceed 70% of the van's value. For an NCV3 with an OM651, replacement is often a no‑brainer — the van is worth fixing. Use our diagnostic calculator to get a personalised verdict.
eras.whyBmwChanged: Path 1 — Know your engine? Launch the Mercedes calculator with Sprinter pre-selected → /fault-diagnostic-calculator?model=sprinter
eras.worthKnowing: Path 2 — Not sure which engine you have? We'll identify it first → /fault-diagnostic-calculator (identification happens as step one inside the tool)

signals.title: Built on Real Data
signals.text: Every reliability rating and cost range on this page comes from genuine UK quote and enquiry data — not manufacturer marketing, not forum guesswork. The OM651, OM642 and OM654 figures are [MB-VERIFIED] from real 2025 UK enquiries.

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: If replacement cost exceeds 70% of what your Sprinter is worth, we say so. That's why the T1N with OM611/OM612 carries a warning — not every site tells you when repair doesn't make financial sense, especially for commercial vehicles where the decision is even more critical.

signals.title: Vetted Mercedes

Every specialist is vetted specifically for Mercedes-Benz work, not generalists who happen to take on Mercedes jobs.

items.question: How much does a Sprinter engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A Sprinter engine replacement costs £1,500–£7,000 supply‑only depending on the generation and engine — an OM651 four‑cylinder diesel for the NCV3/VS30 is £2,800–£4,800 [MB-QUOTE], an OM642 V6 diesel is £3,200–£5,500 [MB-QUOTE], and an OM602 for the T1N is £1,500–£2,800 [MB-QUOTE]. Labour adds 8–12 hours for four‑cylinder units, 10–14 hours for V6 units, at £65–£95/hr (independent commercial van specialist) or £110–£160/hr (Mercedes‑Benz dealer). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised estimate.

items.question: Which Sprinter engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The OM651 2.1‑litre four‑cylinder diesel — fitted to the NCV3 (2009–2018) and early VS30 Sprinters — is the most reliable Sprinter engine. It generated 1,250 UK enquiries in 2025, most of which relate to EGR valve failures, fuel filter housing coolant leaks and glow plug relay issues — all well‑understood, manageable repairs [MB-VERIFIED]. The OM651 is the engine that made the Sprinter the UK's favourite van — its weaknesses are known, predictable and cheaply fixed. Read more about the OM651 →

items.question: Which Sprinter engine should I avoid?
items.answer: Two caveats: * Early OM611/OM612 CDI engines in the T1N — the injectors are prone to failure, and budget £300–£800 per injector to replace them. It's not a reason to avoid the entire van if you're a diy‑mechanic or buying cheap, but factor the cost into your purchase. * OM642 V6 diesel — it's not unreliable, but it's more expensive to maintain and repair than the OM651. The oil cooler seal leak is a labour‑intensive repair (£800–£1,500) and the V6 consumes more fuel. Only choose it if you genuinely need the extra torque for heavy towing or payload.

items.question: How do I know if my Sprinter engine needs replacing?
items.answer: Concrete symptoms by engine: * OM651 diesel (NCV3 / VS30) — EGR valve failure (limp mode, loss of power), fuel filter housing coolant leak (coolant loss with no visible leak), glow plug relay failure (starting issues, electrical faults). These are all repairable — replacement is rarely needed for these specific failures. * OM642 V6 diesel (NCV3 / VS30) — oil leak from the "V" of the engine (oil cooler seals), coolant loss with no visible leak, or oil in the coolant. The oil cooler seal is the primary risk — catch it early and it's a £800–£1,500 repair, not a full engine. * OM611/OM612 CDI (T1N) — rough running, smoke, loss of power, injector failure. Injectors are the primary risk — budget accordingly. * OM602 (T1N) — virtually indestructible; if it's running rough, check injection pump and fuel system before assuming engine failure.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a Sprinter engine?
items.answer: Apply the 70% rule: if a reconditioned engine costs 70% or more of your van's current market value, get a scrap valuation before committing. But for commercial vehicles, also factor in down‑time costs, lost revenue, and the cost of replacing the van. * T1N (OM602) — £1,500–£2,800 recon against a £1,500–£5,000 van = 30–70% — borderline to viable depending on condition. * T1N (OM611/OM612) — £1,800–£3,500 recon against a £1,500–£5,000 van = 50–80% — often not worth it. * NCV3 (OM651) — £2,800–£4,800 recon against a £4,000–£18,000 van = 25–70% — usually worth it for well‑maintained examples. * NCV3 (OM642) — £3,200–£5,500 recon against a £4,000–£18,000 van = 25–80% — watch the numbers, but often viable for fleet operators. * VS30 (OM654) — £3,000–£5,000 recon against a £15,000–£60,000+ van = 8–30% — worth replacing. Use our Ownership Decision Tool for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Which Sprinter generation should I buy?
items.answer: Buy a 2013–2018 NCV3 with the OM651 2.1 CDI — it's the most reliable, best‑supported Sprinter you can own. The 2013 facelift brought improved electronics and the OM651 in its most refined form. If you need a V6, choose the OM642 but budget for an oil cooler seal inspection (£800–£1,500) before purchase. For a VS30, choose the OM654 (2021+) and insist on full service history — the early OM651s in the VS30 are the same proven engine but without the latest tech.

items.question: How do I find my Sprinter engine code?
items.answer: Check the following locations: * VIN plate (visible through the windscreen or on the driver's door pillar) — the engine code is encoded in positions 4–8 of the VIN. * Vehicle registration document (V5C) — the engine number is listed in Section 4. * Under‑bonnet sticker — on the slam panel or timing cover, the engine code is often printed on a factory sticker. * Mercedes‑Benz parts catalogue — enter your VIN at a dealer or online parts site to look up the exact engine code. If you're unsure, use our diagnostic calculator — we'll help you identify it as step one.

items.question: Sprinter vs Vito — which is more reliable?
items.answer: They're different classes — the Sprinter is a large van (3.5–5.5 tonnes), the Vito is a medium van (2.8–3.5 tonnes). They share some engines — the OM651 is used in both — but the Sprinter is generally heavier‑duty and more durable. The Vito's OM651 is the same engine but in a lighter chassis with less towing capacity. The Sprinter's OM602 is legendary; the Vito never had that engine. For reliability, choose the Sprinter if you need the payload and durability; choose the Vito if you need a smaller van for city work. Read the full comparison: Sprinter vs Vito →
